title: How to start a side practice while keeping your job
body: The right path for a solo professional starting on the side is to reduce downside first. Keep the job, choose one narrow offer, validate demand with a small number of paying clients, and avoid building heavy infrastructure before revenue exists. The first win is proof that someone will pay for a focused service in your available hours.

title: Employment and licensing guardrails for a side practice
body: Before taking side clients, a professional should review employer restrictions, conflict-of-interest policies, and any licensing or firm-registration requirements tied to their field. The practical rule is simple: keep employer resources and side work completely separate, confirm the work is allowed, and avoid overlapping client relationships or industries if that creates risk.

title: Use the day job as niche insight, not as a client source
body: A side practice should use the operator's current role as market insight, not as a channel to poach business. If someone works in credit, underwriting, finance, or operations, the smarter move is to package that knowledge into a focused service for adjacent buyers. For a CPA path, that might mean loan-ready financials, bookkeeping cleanup before financing, or small-business reporting that helps owners look more credible to lenders.

title: Transition trigger from side income to full-time practice
body: Do not leave a stable job on potential alone. Set a transition trigger in advance: a monthly revenue target, a number of recurring clients, and enough savings to absorb uneven months. A practical side-practice path is evenings and weekends first, then a deliberate transition only when revenue, pipeline, and operating capacity are visible.
